one_line_takeaway: In this first empirical study of quantization's effect on Go package hallucination, 4-bit (but not 8-bit) quantization of Qwen models sharply and non-uniformly raises both the rate of hallucinated shell-install commands and, in some larger models, the vulnerability rate among the packages that remain valid -- making github.com URL squatting the paper's identified primary attack vector.
key_finding: Quantization increases hallucination, with severity scaling sharply with quantization aggressiveness and (inversely) with model size. 8-bit quantization caused only modest PHR shifts (e.g., 0.5B model on Stack Overflow: 40.60% full precision to 43.04% at 8-bit). 4-bit quantization caused often catastrophic PHR increases in small models -- e.g., 0.5B on MBPP: 53.58% (full) to 98.78% (4-bit); 0.5B on SO: 40.60% to 96.11%; 3B on SO: 33.33% to 92.09% -- while some larger models were comparatively resilient or even improved (7B on SO: PHR fell from 34.00% full to 10.21% at 4-bit, the paper's one clear exception/anomaly). For vulnerability rate among valid packages (VPR), the pattern was scale-dependent and non-monotonic rather than uniformly worse: 8-bit changes were mostly small (0.5B on SO: 12.49% to 12.24%; 7B on paraphrase: 10.92% to 10.17%), but 4-bit caused a sharp VPR increase for the 7B model on Stack Overflow (12.89% full to 19.58% at 4-bit), even as its PHR fell -- illustrating the paper's point that fewer, but more concentrated/riskier, valid packages remained. For RQ3 (package-string characterization on the Qwen-1.5B 4-bit configuration only, Table 2), the large majority of generated package strings were URL-style, dominated by github.com paths, and a majority of generated github.com URLs were hallucinated (e.g., SO: 1,868 hallucinated vs. 1,794 existing github.com strings); golang.org-hosted paths were hallucinated far less often (SO: 42 hallucinated vs. 721 existing), and 'filepath'-style strings were almost never real (SO: 0 existing vs. 1,372 hallucinated).
core_contribution: The paper's own novel contribution is the first systematic empirical study of how post-training weight quantization (8-bit and 4-bit via bitsandbytes) affects package hallucination and known-vulnerability risk in shell commands that LLMs generate for installing Go packages. The authors: (1) curated and release three new prompt datasets (Stack Overflow, MBPP-derived, and awesome-go paraphrase) specifically for this task since no such dataset existed; (2) ran five Qwen model sizes (0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B) at full precision, 8-bit, and 4-bit and measured Package Hallucination Rate (PHR = hallucinated packages / all generated packages, checked by whether the URL in the go get/go install command resolves) and Vulnerable Package Rate (VPR = vulnerable / existing packages, checked against the OSV.dev API); (3) characterized the string patterns of hallucinated packages (URL vs. filepath vs. other, github.com vs. golang.org vs. other-URL) for one representative configuration (Qwen-1.5B, 4-bit).
threat_model: Stated explicitly in the paper's own Section 2 (not inherited from cited work): two parties -- a developer who blindly trusts and executes shell commands an LLM generates to install Go packages, and an attacker who can observe which package names/URLs the LLM tends to hallucinate and pre-register a matching malicious package (a Go-ecosystem instance of 'slopsquatting', citing Spracklen et al. and a Trend Micro white paper for the term but applying it to their own Go/shell-command setting). The paper's Discussion section (Sec. 7) extends this with two attack vectors the authors themselves reason through: (a) squatting hallucinated github.com URLs, since anyone can create a matching username/repo, and (b) compromising existing, frequently-recommended real packages (e.g., via malicious PRs or leaked GitHub tokens) since LLMs also repeatedly suggest the same valid packages. They also raise (speculatively, flagged as 'another unexplored area' and not evaluated) training-data poisoning to bias the LLM toward attacker-controlled package names.
limitations: Self-reported by the authors in Section 8 (Threats to Validity and Future Improvements): hallucination detection relied only on HTTP/network reachability of the embedded URL rather than actually attempting `go get`, which the authors acknowledge can misclassify packages (a reachable URL is not proof of a valid, installable Go module, and vice versa) -- they explicitly list executing real go-get commands as future work. Prompt diversity/robustness (e.g., varying prompt phrasing) was not systematically tested and is listed as future work. RQ3's string-pattern characterization was reported for only one model/quantization configuration (Qwen-1.5B, 4-bit) 'because the numbers from all models and quantization methods would create an enormous table,' so the generality of that specific breakdown across other sizes/precisions is not directly evidenced in the paper. The study is scoped to a single model family (Qwen) and a single ecosystem/language (Go), and to only two quantization schemes (8-bit and 4-bit via bitsandbytes) -- no other model families or quantization methods (e.g., GPTQ, AWQ) were tested. The 3B model's very small package counts at full precision and 8-bit (e.g., 3 and 1 generated Go packages respectively on Stack Overflow, 0 on MBPP at both precisions) make its Table 1 percentages statistically fragile, though the paper does not flag this itself.

datasets_used: Stack Overflow (SO): 5,435 golang-tagged questions filtered to those needing package installation, derived from an initial 10,157 via GPT-4o task/command generation and filtering
MBPP-derived: 974 prompts adapted from the MBPP text-to-code benchmark, converted from Python-specific to language-generic prompts
Paraphrase: 1,746 prompts generated by GPT-4o by paraphrasing package names/metadata scraped from the awesome-go repository
quantitative_results: PHR, 7B model, Stack Overflow: 34.00% (full) -> 34.06% (8-bit) -> 10.21% (4-bit) -- the paper's noted exception where 4-bit reduced hallucination

Hallucination string distribution (Table 2, Qwen-1.5B 4-bit, Stack Overflow set): github.com URLs 1,794 existing vs. 1,868 hallucinated; golang.org URLs 721 existing vs. 42 hallucinated; filepath-style strings 0 existing vs. 1,372 hallucinated

Discussion-section compression figures: 8-bit quantization yields 3.1-3.6x compression with <2.5% PHR change for most model sizes except 3B; 4-bit yields 4.2-5.7x compression but +55.5% PHR increase for 0.5B and +58.8% for 3B, versus -23.8% (improvement) for 7B and +6.6% for 14B
baselines_compared: No external baseline systems are compared; the study's comparison is internal -- each Qwen model size's full-precision (32-bit) variant serves as the baseline against which its own 8-bit and 4-bit quantized variants are measured, across the five model sizes (0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B) and three datasets (SO, MBPP, Paraphrase)
